sections.paragraph:
βρύχω impf. ἔβρυχον; fut. 3 sg. βρύξει; 1 aor. 3 sg. ἔβρυξεν LXX to grind one’s teeth, gnash, a sign of violent rage (Theodor. Prodr. 5, 49 H.) τοὺς ὀδόντας ἐπʼ αὐτόν they gnashed their teeth against him Ac 7:54 (cp. Lex. Vind. p. 34, 5 βρύχει τ. ὀδόντας ἄνθρωπος, βρυχᾶται δὲ λέων=humans gnash their teeth, lions roar; Job 16:9; Ps 34:16; 36:12; SibOr 2, 203; Hippocr., 8 p. 16 [Mul. 1, 2], Epid. 5, 86 and other medical wr. [Hobart 208] of chattering of the teeth in chills and fevers). The expression may also be intended as metaph. become enraged (so NRSV with others, unless the translators are thinking in terms of functional equivalence).—Frisk.

impf. impf. = imperfect

fut. fut. = fut.

  1. sg. = singular

aor. aor. = aorist

LXX LXX = Septuaginta, ed. ARahlfs, unless otherwise specified—Lists 2, beg.

  1. H. = FHort, s. W-H.—List 1, beg.
  2. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

Lex. Vind. Lex. Vind. = Lexicon Vindobenense—List 5

SibOr SibOr = Oracula Sibyllina, II–III a.d.—List 5

Hippocr Hippocr = Corpus Hippocraticum, V–IV b.c.—List 5

  1. wr. = writers, writings

Hobart Hobart = WH., Medical Language of St. Luke—List 6

metaph. metaph. = metaphor(ically)

NRSV NRSV = New Revised Standard Version of the NT—List 6

Frisk Frisk = HF., Griechisches etymologisches Wörterbuch—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 184
